request.question: Who is able to fill a larger variety of roles in the entertainment industry, Dave Moody or Ransom Riggs?

title: Ransom Riggs
Library of Souls
No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School
Dave Moody
Hollow City (novel)
Season of Miracles
Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children
Entertainment industry in India
The Moody Brothers
Praise Band: The Movie
sentences: Ransom Riggs (born February 3, 1979) is an American writer and filmmaker best known for the book "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children".
Library of Souls is a sequel to 2014 novel "Hollow City" written by Ransom Riggs and third book in the series of "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children".
It was released on September 22, 2015 by Quirk Books.
No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School is a 2010 direct-to-video family comedy film.
It starred Bill Cobbs, Lee Meriwether, Blake Michael, Celeste Kellogg and Ashton Harrell.
The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was directed by Dave Moody for Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures, who also provided the musical score for the film.
David B. Moody is an artist, producer, songwriter and filmmaker from North Carolina.
His instrumental proficiency has earned him two Grammy Award Nominations and three International CMA Awards as a member of The Moody Brothers.

After becoming regular performers on the Grand Ole Opry and touring extensively throughout Europe in the 80s, the trio of Carolina brothers signed a contract with the Walt Disney Company in 1992 to open and perform their own nightly concerts at Disneyland Paris' Disney Village, where they performed for over 50 million guests during their time in France.
Hollow City is a 2014 dark fantasy novel and a sequel to "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children" written by Ransom Riggs.
It was released on January 14, 2014 by Quirk Books.
The novel is set right after the first, and sees Jacob and his friends fleeing from Miss Peregrine's to the "peculiar capital of the world", London.
Season of Miracles is a 2013 American Christian-themed dramatic film directed by Dave Moody and starring John Schneider, Grayson Russell, Andrew Williams, and Nancy Stafford.

Based on writer Rusty Whitener's award winning novel "A Season of Miracles", the story is set in Alabama during the 1970s and follows a group of twelve-year-olds and their championship little league baseball season.
Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children is a contemporary fantasy debut novel by American author Ransom Riggs.
The story is told through a combination of narrative and vernacular photographs from the personal archives of collectors listed by the author.
The media and entertainment industry in India consists of many different segments under its folds such as television, print, and films.
It also includes smaller segments like radio, music, OOH, animation, gaming and visual effects (VFX) and Internet advertising.
Entertainment industry in India has registered an explosive growth in last two decades making it one of the fastest growing industries in India.
From a single state owned channel, Doordarshan in the 1990s there are more than 400 active channels in the country.
Worldwide, 2010 saw the global economy begin to recover from a steep decline in 2009.
Improved economic conditions in 2010 played a major role in a rebound in customer spend.
Since the world economy begin to recover from the global financial crisis of 2008, improved economic conditions played a major role in rebound in consumer spend.
While India was not critically impacted by the downturn in 2008 and 2009, it demonstrated one of the highest growth rates this year and continued to at a healthy pace.
The rising rate of investments by the private sector and foreign media and entertainment (M&E) majors have improved India's entertainment infrastructure to a great extent.
As per the recent report by PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC), Indians are likely to spend more on entertainment in the coming years with a steady growth in their disposable income.
And as per the combined survey report by KMPG and FICCI, the entertainment industry in India is expected to expand by 12.5% every year and is likely to reach US$20.09 billion by the year 2013.
The industry pegged at INR 5808 billion in 2009 as compared to INR 3565 billion in 2005.

The Indian Media & Entertainment Industry grew from US$12.9 billion in 2009 to US$14.4 billion in 2010, a growth of 11 per cent, according to a report by the Federation of Indian Chambers of Commerce and Industry (FICCI) and research firm KPMG.
The report also states that backed by positive industry sentiment and growing media consumption, the industry is estimated to achieve growth of 13 per cent in 2011 to touch US$16.2 billion.
As the industry braces for exciting times ahead, the sector is projected to grow at a CAGR of 14 percent to reach US$28.1 billion by 2015.

The Moody Brothers are an Americana country music trio who gained prominence in 1985 when they were nominated for a Grammy Award for their instrumental performance of the classic fiddle tune "Cotton Eyed Joe".
This trio of brothers, Carlton Moody, Dave Moody, and Trent Moody were nominated for a second Grammy in 1990 and won three International Country Music Awards in Europe.
Praise Band: The Movie is a 2008 faith-based film by Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures.
It starred George Hamilton IV, Adam Melton and James Dana Bryan.
The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was produced and directed by Dave Moody.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Who is able to fill a larger variety of roles in the entertainment industry, Dave Moody or Ransom Riggs?
hop1_docs: Ransom Riggs | Ransom Riggs (born February 3, 1979) is an American writer and filmmaker best known for the book "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children".

No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School | No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School is a 2010 direct-to-video family comedy film.  It starred Bill Cobbs, Lee Meriwether, Blake Michael, Celeste Kellogg and Ashton Harrell.  The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was directed by Dave Moody for Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures, who also provided the musical score for the film.

Praise Band: The Movie | Praise Band: The Movie is a 2008 faith-based film by Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures.  It starred George Hamilton IV, Adam Melton and James Dana Bryan.  The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was produced and directed by Dave Moody.

The Moody Brothers | The Moody Brothers are an Americana country music trio who gained prominence in 1985 when they were nominated for a Grammy Award for their instrumental performance of the classic fiddle tune "Cotton Eyed Joe".  This trio of brothers, Carlton Moody, Dave Moody, and Trent Moody were nominated for a second Grammy in 1990 and won three International Country Music Awards in Europe.

Dave Moody (sportscaster) | David Wayne "Dave" Moody is an American motorsports commentator who works for the Motor Racing Network.  He is the lead turn announcer for MRN and has worked for the radio network on and off since 1983.  He moved to MRN full-time in 1997 and became the lead turn announcer in 2001.  Since 2003, Moody has been the host of the nationally syndicated Sirius XM show "Sirius XM Speedway" on Sirius XM NASCAR Radio.
Dave Nutting | David Judd Nutting is an industrial design engineer who played a role in the early video game industry.  He is a graduate of the Pratt Institute with a degree in industrial design.

Ransomes Industrial Estate | Ransomes Industrial Estate, also known as Ransomes Europark is combined retail and business park on the South-Eastern edge of Ipswich in Suffolk, UK.  It takes its name from Ransomes, Sims & Jefferies, which still maintains a presence on the park.
hop1_titles: No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School
response.summary_1:
Evidence: passages call Ransom Riggs an "American writer and filmmaker" and list Dave Moody in film/music roles (director, producer, composer, band member) and also a separate Dave Moody as a motorsports "lead turn announcer."  
Bridge entity: Ransom Riggs; Dave Moody (ambiguous — both the filmmaker/musician Dave Moody and Dave Moody (sportscaster) appear).  
Missing fact: the complete list/count of occupations for each person (which one holds a larger variety of entertainment-industry roles) is not provided in the passages.  
Retrieval directions: (1) open Ransom Riggs's biography (e.g., Wikipedia or official site) to extract his full occupations/roles; (2) open the Dave Moody biography pages — at minimum the Dave Moody linked to Elevating Entertainment/The Moody Brothers and the Dave Moody (sportscaster) page — to extract each one's full occupations/roles and then compare.  
Clarification: if you meant a specific Dave Moody, please say whether you mean the filmmaker/musician Dave Moody or Dave Moody the motorsports announcer.
response.hop2_query:
Search and open biography pages and authoritative profiles for:
1) Ransom Riggs — open Wikipedia and his official site/author page. Extract every occupation/role listed (exact phrasing), e.g., writer, novelist, filmmaker, director, screenwriter, producer, actor, photographer, etc. Note sources and provide direct links. 
2) Dave Moody (filmmaker/musician) — open his Wikipedia or official site (e.g., Dave Moody musician, The Moody Brothers, Elevating Entertainment). Extract every occupation/role listed (director, producer, composer, musician, band member, studio owner, record producer, songwriter, etc.). Provide links and citations.
3) Dave Moody (motorsports announcer) — open his Wikipedia or authoritative bio (e.g., track/series site). Extract every occupation/role listed related to entertainment/broadcasting (announcer, commentator, broadcaster, emcee). Provide links and citations.

For each person/page:
- Return a concise list of distinct occupations/roles (one-line each).
- Count the number of distinct entertainment-industry roles.
- If any roles are ambiguous (e.g., "filmmaker" vs "director/writer/producer"), list the detailed sub-roles shown on the page.
Finally:
- Compare counts and state which individual (Ransom Riggs vs which Dave Moody) fills a larger variety of entertainment-industry roles. If the user likely meant a specific Dave Moody, note that and provide the comparison for both Dave Moodys if ambiguous.
hop2_docs: Dave Moody | David B. Moody is an artist, producer, songwriter and filmmaker from North Carolina.  His instrumental proficiency has earned him two Grammy Award Nominations and three International CMA Awards as a member of The Moody Brothers.  After becoming regular performers on the Grand Ole Opry and touring extensively throughout Europe in the 80s, the trio of Carolina brothers signed a contract with the Walt Disney Company in 1992 to open and perform their own nightly concerts at Disneyland Paris' Disney Village, where they performed for over 50 million guests during their time in France.

Praise Band: The Movie | Praise Band: The Movie is a 2008 faith-based film by Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures.  It starred George Hamilton IV, Adam Melton and James Dana Bryan.  The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was produced and directed by Dave Moody.

Dave Moody (sportscaster) | David Wayne "Dave" Moody is an American motorsports commentator who works for the Motor Racing Network.  He is the lead turn announcer for MRN and has worked for the radio network on and off since 1983.  He moved to MRN full-time in 1997 and became the lead turn announcer in 2001.  Since 2003, Moody has been the host of the nationally syndicated Sirius XM show "Sirius XM Speedway" on Sirius XM NASCAR Radio.

No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School | No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School is a 2010 direct-to-video family comedy film.  It starred Bill Cobbs, Lee Meriwether, Blake Michael, Celeste Kellogg and Ashton Harrell.  The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was directed by Dave Moody for Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures, who also provided the musical score for the film.

David Burris | David Burris (sometimes credited as Dave Burris or David Allen Burris) is an American filmmaker, television producer, writer and musician.  He directed the feature film "The World Made Straight" ("Millennium Entertainment") and the short film "The Side of the Road", which he also wrote.  He was the executive producer on CBS's "Survivor" and a writer on the USA/SyFy series "Good vs. Evil".  As a musician, he was a founding member and songwriter for Sire/Warner recording artists Jolene and a recording and touring member of Mercury/Polygram recording artists The Veldt.

Ben Moody | Benjamin Robert "Ben" Moody (born January 22, 1981) is an American musician, singer, songwriter, record producer, and actor.  He is best known as co-founder, lead guitarist, and co-songwriter of rock band Evanescence from its inception in 1995 to his departure in October 2003.  Since leaving Evanescence, Moody has collaborated with an array of performers vocally, instrumentally, and as a writer.  He is currently a member of The Halo Method and We Are The Fallen.

Keys of the Kingdom | Keys of the Kingdom is the fourteenth album by the rock band The Moody Blues, released in 1991.  Although some of the tracks recall the songwriting on "Sur la Mer", the failure of "Keys of the Kingdom" to produce any major hit singles would mark the beginning of the Moodies' decline in popularity with mainstream audiences after their success in the MTV video generation.  The album saw the band beginning to return to a more airy rock orientated sound (similar to "The Present"), rather than the previous two albums' forays into synthesiser pop.  Flautist Ray Thomas plays more of a substantial role on this record, with his first ambient flute piece in eight years.  The album was recorded and mixed at four London studios: Olympic Studios, RG Jones Studios, Mayfair Studios and The Hit Factory (not to be confused with the NYC-based studio of the same name).  Drum machines are still used heavily; Graeme Edge only performs on three songs, with session drummer Andy Duncan providing live drums on two more, and the rest being programmed.  Keyboardist Patrick Moraz was fired from the band after completing only a few tracks for this album, and he is listed as an 'additional' keyboard player.  After a lawsuit on Court TV filed by Moraz, their future album and re-release booklets would distance themselves from Moraz - e.g. removing him from photographs that originally featured him - and they never had any future keyboardists become official members of the band.
hop2_titles: No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School
response.summary_2: Dave Moody can fill a larger variety of entertainment roles than Ransom Riggs. Passages show Dave Moody works as an artist, producer, songwriter, filmmaker and film director/composer (and was a member of The Moody Brothers), whereas Ransom Riggs is described only as an American writer and filmmaker, so Moody holds a broader set of entertainment-industry roles.
